event_blocks.summary: A young man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, seated in a modern room with blue-purple lighting, begins by humorously denying playing Mahjong Soul for anime waifus, referencing its official ad, before conceding their presence. He then passionately explains his love for Riichi Mahjong, citing low self-esteem and the game's rewarding risk, and announces a tutorial for YouTube and Twitch. A split-screen displays a 'Riichi Mahjong' collage with anime characters and tiles, including 'Nadeshiko' with 'Big Three Dragons,' while he concludes the segment with 'PowerPoint.' He clarifies he plays Riichi Mahjong on Mahjong Soul, noting its availability and cosmetic purchases. The scene shifts to a chalkboard-style 'Mahjong Tiles' tutorial screen where Ichihime introduces the 34 unique tiles. The young man elaborates on the three suits (character, circles, bamboo) numbered one through nine, and honor tiles (winds and dragons), emphasizing these are in-game assets and a full set has 136 tiles. Transitioning to an overhead 3D Mahjong table, he explains real-life tile arrangement before showing an in-game interface of Mahjong Soul with hidden walls. He details the draw-and-discard loop, then moves to tutorial screens on winning hands, explaining pairs, triplets, and sequences, using examples like '3, 4, 5 of the circle suit.' Ichihime illustrates 'Triplets and Sequences,' and the young man provides examples like '789 of the character suit,' clarifying that '9 is the highest' and sequences cannot wrap around. He explains a winning hand requires '4 Sequences or Triplets + 1 Pair,' emphasizing the flexibility of combinations and the necessity of a pair. A quiz appears asking, 'Which tile does the hand need for winning?' He analyzes a hand with a pair of 'two of characters' and sequences, identifying the need for a 'two of circles' to win, but selecting option A (2-Pin) results in Ichihime's 'Try another one-nya!' The hand resets to a new configuration with a sequence of 1-9 Pin tiles, three 1-Flower tiles, and a 2-Sou tile, prompting the young man to remark, 'Uh, okay, this one's a little trickier,' as he begins to analyze the new hand, identifying 'the one, two, three of circles' as one sequence. Throughout, his room features a dark, grid-patterned background with ambient blue-purple lighting, shelves, and decorative items.

Young Man.description: A young man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, speaking into a microphone with an analytical and engaging voice. He actively analyzes the mahjong puzzles, verbalizing his thought process, identifying tile sequences and pairs, and expressing his conclusions with a confident and occasionally humorous tone, such as when he calls a tile arrangement 'kind of sus' or confidently declares his winning tile choice. His expressions shift from thoughtful to slightly amused as he processes the puzzles.

Mahjong Soul Animation.description: A colorful, festive animated sequence from the game 'Mahjong Soul' featuring steamed buns with cute faces, red clouds, and the text 'Festive Aroma' in glowing script, with a music credit for 'Splendid Dreams' from the 'A Feast for Nian Event.' The Young Man mentions 'Mahjong Soul' as the primary platform he uses for playing Riichi Mahjong, highlighting its free availability across various platforms and optional cosmetic purchases.

Riichi Mahjong Collage.description: A split-screen graphic titled 'Riichi Mahjong' displaying anime-style characters, mahjong tiles, and the name 'Nadeshiko' with 'Big Three Dragons,' alongside a close-up of a character with an eyepatch and the word 'RIICHI,' with the young man in a small inset window reacting to the content.

3D Mahjong Table.description: A 3D-rendered overhead view of a mahjong table with tiles arranged in walls, set on a teal playing surface with black borders, visually representing the 'real life' mahjong setup described by the young man.

Mahjong Tiles Tutorial Screen.description: An in-game tutorial screen titled 'Which tile does the hand need for winning?' displayed on a chalkboard-style interface, featuring a hand of mahjong tiles and three multiple-choice options (A, B, C) below, with the chibi character Ichihime providing instructions and feedback. The Young Man reads the title aloud, reinforcing its presence.

In-Game Mahjong Interface.description: A digital mahjong game interface with a blue table, player avatars labeled 'AI(Easy)' and 'Yu_kong' at the four corners, a hand of tiles at the bottom, a central score display showing 'East 1' and '25000' points, and the number '293' on the right side of the screen, representing the online 'Mahjong Soul' gameplay environment described by the young man.

Ichihime.description: A chibi-style anime character with brown hair, cat ears, red eyes, and a red-and-white traditional outfit, appearing on the left side of the tutorial screen, providing explanations with a cheerful demeanor and labeled 'Ichihime' in a red banner.

raw_segments: The video opens with a young man, characterized by his short black hair, round glasses, and light pink button-up shirt, seated in a modern, cozy room with ambient blue-purple lighting, speaking animatedly into a professional microphone. He uses expressive hand gestures and wide-eyed smiles as he declares, "First off, and I just want to get this out of the way, I'm not playing Mahjong for the anime waifus, okay? Mahjong Soul is not a game about anime waifus." He challenges viewers to "just take a look at their official ad." The scene then cuts to a vibrant, festive animated sequence from the game 'Mahjong Soul,' showcasing steamed buns with cute faces and the text 'Festive Aroma' in glowing script, accompanied by the cheerful background music 'Splendid Dreams' from the 'A Feast for Nian Event.' The video returns to the man, who, with a slight chuckle, concedes, "Okay, there are some anime waifus in the game." He then shifts to a more serious tone, stating his love for Riichi Mahjong and explaining why, revealing, "I've got low self-esteem." He passionately describes the game's risky yet valuable hands, the need to "believe in yourself and fight for a chance to win," and the fun of playing "without regrets." He announces his intention to create "a tutorial for the very basics of Riichi Mahjong" for his YouTube videos and Twitch audience, explaining the rules of the game. As he continues speaking with energy, occasionally pointing, a split-screen appears, displaying a collage titled 'Riichi Mahjong' featuring anime-style characters and mahjong tiles, including 'Nadeshiko' with 'Big Three Dragons,' while the man appears in a small inset window, still smiling and reacting to the content. He concludes the segment by saying, "PowerPoint."

The video opens with a split-screen 'Riichi Mahjong' collage, showcasing anime characters and tiles, as the Young Man, in a small inset, reacts with expressive gestures and a lighthearted 'it's PowerPoint.' He then explains that he primarily plays Riichi Mahjong on 'Mahjong Soul,' a game whose vibrant title screen, featuring anime-style female characters and cherry blossoms, is displayed, noting it's 'available for free in web browsers, iOS, or Android,' with optional cosmetic purchases. The scene transitions to a chalkboard-style 'Mahjong Tiles' tutorial screen, where a chibi character, Ichihime, introduces the 34 unique tiles. The Young Man, gesturing towards the screen, elaborates on the tile types, explaining there are three main suits—character tiles (Man), circles (Pin), and bamboo (Sou)—each numbered one through nine, and honor tiles, which include the four winds (East, South, West, North) and the three dragons (White, Green, Red). He emphasizes that these tutorial images are from the game itself and suggests viewers complete it. He concludes by stating that a full Mahjong set contains 136 tiles, with four copies of each, before the view shifts to an overhead 3D-rendered mahjong table with tiles arranged for gameplay, as he begins to explain further.

The video opens with a 3D-rendered overhead view of a mahjong table, showcasing neatly arranged tile walls on a teal playing surface, as a young man with a clear, engaging male voice explains how tiles would be arranged in "real life" mahjong. He then transitions to an in-game interface of a digital mahjong match, displaying a blue table with player avatars 'AI(Easy)' and 'Yu_kong', a hand of tiles, and a central score display, noting that this is how online play in "Mahjong Soul" typically looks since the wall structure is hidden. The young man, seated in a cozy room with ambient blue-purple lighting, continues to explain the basic gameplay loop of drawing and discarding tiles, using expressive hand gestures and facial expressions. As he introduces how to win, the scene shifts to a tutorial screen titled 'How to win a hand: Pairs,' featuring a chibi character 'Ichihime' on a chalkboard-style interface. The young man's voice defines a pair as "two of the same tile," adding a humorous remark about players who might need help with this concept. He then moves to the next tutorial screen, 'How to win a hand: Triplets and Sequences,' where Ichihime illustrates these concepts. The young man explains that triplets are self-explanatory but sequences are "a little trickier," defining them as "a series of three consecutive tiles from the same suit," providing an example of "3, 4, 5 of the circle suit," all while light, upbeat game music plays in the background.

The segment opens on a mahjong game tutorial screen, titled 'How to win a hand: Triplets and Sequences,' presented on a chalkboard-style interface. A chibi character, Ichihime, with brown hair, cat ears, and a red-and-white outfit, stands to the left, visually explaining the rules. The Young Man, visible in a small inset window, actively engages, nodding and pointing as he articulates examples of sequences, stating, "789 of the character suit and 567 of the bamboo suit." He further clarifies that "9 is the highest of any suit, we can't go above that, like a sequence can't be 891." He enthusiastically declares, "If we can combine those concepts together, we can win." The tutorial then transitions to a 'Winning' screen, illustrating that a winning hand requires '4 Sequences or Triplets + 1 Pair.' The Young Man continues to explain, "So, in order to win, you need four sequences or triplets and one pair." He elaborates on the flexibility of combinations, noting, "The wording for four sequences or triplets can be a little confusing, so let me explain. You can have any combination of those. For example, a winning hand could be two sequences and two triplets. Another winning hand could have four sequences and no triplets." He emphasizes, "but in both of those examples, you still need a pair, in order to complete your winning hand." The screen then shifts to a quiz, asking, 'Which tile does the hand need for winning?' displaying a hand of tiles and three multiple-choice options. The Young Man, gesturing and speaking into his microphone, reacts with enthusiasm, saying, "Okay, great. So, the tutorial has some like little test questions... So let's try to answer this together. So which tile does the hand need for winning? Uh, let's take a look at it. So, first off, we got a pair, it seems, of the two of characters. That seems pretty good. That seems..." as he analyzes the options. The camera briefly zooms in on the hand of tiles, highlighting the arrangement and the question mark placeholder. Throughout, the Young Man's room maintains a consistent dark, grid-patterned background with ambient blue-purple lighting, featuring shelves, decorative items, and a keyboard.

The segment opens on a digital mahjong tutorial screen, presented on a teal chalkboard-style interface, posing the question 'Which tile does the hand need for winning?' The hand displays various mahjong tiles, including red 'Man', black 'Pin', green 'Sou', and a green dragon. Below, three options are visible: A. a 2-Pin tile, B. a 3-Sou tile, and C. an East Wind tile. On the left, the chibi character Ichihime, with brown hair, cat ears, and a red-and-white outfit, provides the instruction: 'Make sure your hand becomes 4 Sequences or Triplets + 1 Pair nyaa!' In the bottom right, a Young Man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, speaks into a microphone, actively analyzing the mahjong hand. He articulates his thought process, identifying a pair and sequences, stating, "looks like we've got our pair taken care of," and then pointing out, "we have the four, the five, and the six of characters... That seems like a sequence to me." He continues to break down the hand, noting a "three of circles and a four of circles here," and humorously concluding, "It's not enough for a sequence, so this is looking kind of sus." He then identifies bamboo sequences, summarizing, "in total we have our pair, we have one sequence, two sequences, three sequences, and we need to finish this one." After posing, "So, what do we need to win?" he confidently declares, "It's gonna be the two of circles. That's the tile that we need to finish this hand." He then selects option A (2-Pin), which prompts Ichihime's visual feedback: 'Try another one-nya!' The hand on the screen immediately resets to a new configuration, now showing a sequence of 1-9 Pin tiles, three 1-Flower tiles, and a 2-Sou tile, with the same three options. The Young Man, his expression shifting from thoughtful to slightly amused, continues his analysis, reading the new puzzle title, "Which tile does the hand need for winning?" and remarking, "Uh, okay, this one's a little trickier, so let's let's go through it," as he begins to identify sequences in the new hand, such as "the one, two, three of circles... That's one sequence." The background maintains a dark, grid-patterned digital interface with ambient blue-purple lighting throughout.

event_blocks.summary: The 'Mahjong Soul' tutorial begins with a chalkboard-style interface presenting a winning hand scenario, where the chibi character Ichihime, adorned in a traditional outfit with cat ears, prompts the player with 'Try another one-nya!'. In his ambient blue-purple lit room, a Young Man, wearing a pink shirt and round glasses, analyzes the hand into a microphone, explaining the objective of forming four sequences/triplets and a pair, noting the absence of a pair. He dismisses the 'two of circles' and identifies the 'three of bamboo' as the winning tile, completing both a pair and a sequence. The tutorial shifts to 'Calling Tiles: Pon,' featuring a 3D mahjong table with 'AI(Easy)' and 'Yu_kong' avatars. The Young Man explains that a 'Pon' call is used to complete a triplet when a player possesses a pair (like two 9s of characters) and another player discards the matching tile, with Ichihime clarifying 'Pon is the call to complete a Triplet.' A 'Pon' button and a 12-second countdown appear. Ichihime, now with brown hair, cat ears, and a red-and-white outfit, cheerfully explains the 'Pon' rule in a separate 'Calling Tiles: Pon' tutorial, while the Young Man, in an inset window, elaborates on completing triplets by taking a discarded tile. The scene transitions to the 'Calling Tiles: Chii' tutorial, where the Young Man introduces 'Chii' for completing sequences. A green circle highlights a 4 of bamboo tile, presenting 'Chii' options and a countdown. Ichihime explains 'Chii' is for sequences using the last player's discard. The Young Man provides an example: holding a 6 and 8 of circles, he can 'Chii' a discarded 7 of circles from the player to his left to complete a sequence, emphasizing 'Chii' is only for sequences and only from the left player. He then playfully transitions to a quiz, asking if the material will be on the prelim, and announcing 'Quiz time.' The quiz question, 'Which of the following hands can Chii the 6 of bamboo from the last player?', appears with options A, B, and C. Ichihime encourages the player with 'If you answer the question correctly, you can have my snack-nya!' The Young Man begins analyzing the quiz, noting that 'Chii means we're completing a sequence here.'

Young Man.description: A young man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, speaking into a microphone with expressive gestures and facial expressions in a cozy, modern room with shelves, decorative items, and ambient blue-purple lighting, whose voice is heard throughout the segment as he explains Mahjong rules, provides examples, asks rhetorical questions, and analyzes a quiz.

Mahjong Tiles Tutorial Screen.description: An in-game tutorial screen titled 'Which tile does the hand need for winning?' displayed on a chalkboard-style interface, featuring a hand of mahjong tiles and three multiple-choice options (A, B, C) below, with the chibi character Ichihime providing instructions and feedback.

raw_segments: The segment opens on a 'Mahjong Soul' tutorial screen, 'Which tile does the hand need for winning?', presented on a chalkboard-style interface with a mahjong hand and three tile options. The chibi character Ichihime, with cat ears and a traditional outfit, encourages the player with 'Try another one-nya!' on the left. A Young Man, wearing a pink shirt and round glasses, speaks into a microphone in his ambient blue-purple lit room, analyzing the hand aloud. He notes the hand's trickiness, stating, "we don't have our pair yet," and explains the goal of forming four sequences/triplets and one pair. He dismisses the 'two of circles' option, then focuses on the 'three of bamboo,' explaining how it would complete a pair and a sequence, concluding enthusiastically, "it is the three of bamboo that we need to win!" The screen transitions to a 'Calling Tiles: Pon' tutorial, showing a 3D mahjong table with 'AI(Easy)' and 'Yu_kong' avatars. The Young Man continues, discussing strategies and explaining that if you have a pair, like the two 9s of characters highlighted on screen, and another player discards that same tile, "you can call Pon." Ichihime's text clarifies that 'Pon is the call to complete a Triplet,' as the Young Man nods and gestures, with a 'Pon' button and a '12' second countdown visible on the interface.

The video opens with a 'Calling Tiles: Pon' tutorial screen from 'Mahjong Soul,' displaying a 3D mahjong table with 'AI(Easy)' avatars and a hand of tiles. A blue circle highlights a 9 of characters tile, indicating a 'Pon' call with 'Pon' or 'Skip' options and a countdown. The chibi character Ichihime, with brown hair, cat ears, red eyes, and a red-and-white traditional outfit, appears on the left, cheerfully explaining the rule. Simultaneously, a Young Man, with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, is seen in a small inset window, speaking into a microphone with expressive gestures in a cozy, blue-purple lit room, explaining how to "reveal the two that you have and then take their tile to complete your triplet set," assuring viewers an example will be shown later. The scene then transitions to the 'Calling Tiles: Chii' tutorial, where the Young Man announces, "Next, let's talk about Chii, which is how we can complete sequences." The screen now shows a green circle highlighting a 4 of bamboo tile, with the 'Chii' option and a countdown. Ichihime continues to explain the 'Chii' rule, emphasizing its use for completing a sequence with the last player's discarded tile. The Young Man elaborates on an example, stating, "in this example, we have the six of circles and the eight of circles and the player to our left has discarded the seven of circles, which is exactly what we need. So, we can Chii their tile, reveal our six and eight, and use their seven to complete a sequence." He cautions that 'Chii' can only be used for sequences and only when the player to the left discards the needed tile, not the player across or to the right. He then playfully asks, "Uh, you song, is any of this gonna be on the prelim? Yes, it's gonna be on the prelim. Quiz time," as the tutorial presents a quiz question: 'Which of the following hands can Chii the 6 of bamboo from the last player?' with three multiple-choice options (A, B, C) displayed below. Ichihime encourages the player with the text 'If you answer the question correctly, you can have my snack-nya!' The Young Man continues to engage with the content, gesturing and speaking into the microphone, maintaining his expressive demeanor while beginning to analyze the quiz, noting, "Chii means we're completing a sequence here. And so this one's starting to look good."

event_blocks.summary: The Mahjong tutorial begins with a screen asking which hands can Chii the 6 of bamboo, where the Young Man explains sequence formation rules, identifying 'A, the 4, 5, and 6 of bamboo' as correct. The next question, 'Which of the following hands can Pon the West wind from another player?', is presented, and the Young Man explains 'Pon' as forming a triplet set, selecting 'C' as the answer. The tutorial then shifts to 'A winning hand must contain a Yaku,' with Ichihime and the Young Man elaborating that Yaku are necessary win conditions beyond sets and a pair, with the Young Man admitting to simplifying previous explanations. A subsequent screen, 'Simple Yaku: Yakuhai,' displays Dragon and Wind triplets as Yaku, and the Young Man clarifies that triplets of Dragons, the Prevalent Wind (East), or the Seat Wind (West, if applicable) constitute a Yaku. The tutorial moves to 'Simple Yaku: Tanyao,' humorously renamed 'All Simps,' which disallows terminal or honor tiles, with the Young Man advising new players to aim for it. A quiz follows: 'Which of the following Sequences is a Yaku?', and the Young Man correctly identifies 'A' (three East Wind tiles) as the answer, noting it's both the prevalent and seat wind. Another quiz, 'Which tile should you discard if you want to achieve Tanyao?', is presented, and the Young Man explains that tiles 1, 9, and honor tiles must be avoided, humorously deeming the '1 of Bamboo' as 'sus' and the correct discard.

Young Man.description: A young man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, speaking into a microphone with an engaging and analytical voice, making expressive gestures and facial expressions in a cozy, modern room with shelves, decorative items, and ambient blue-purple lighting. He actively vocalizes his thought process, explaining Mahjong rules, answering quiz questions, and reacting humorously to tile options, notably mispronouncing 'simples' as 'simps' and exclaiming 'in this economy?' when encountering a '1' tile.

Ichihime.description: A chibi-style anime character with brown hair, cat ears, red eyes, and a red-and-white traditional outfit, positioned on the left side of the screen, providing explanations with a cheerful demeanor and labeled 'Ichihime' in a red banner.

Mahjong Tiles Tutorial Screen.description: An in-game tutorial screen with a chalkboard-style interface, displaying two quiz questions: first, 'Which of the following Sequences is a Yaku?' with options A (three East Wind tiles), B (three South Wind tiles), and C (three West Wind tiles), and second, 'Which tile should you discard if you want to achieve Tanyao?' with options A (1 of Bamboo), B (7 of Bamboo), and C (3 of Circles). The score display shows 'East 1' and '25000' points, and the text includes hints about Yaku and Tanyao rules, which the Young Man reads aloud and uses for his detailed analysis.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a mahjong tutorial screen, titled 'Which of the following hands can Chii the 6 of bamboo from the last player?', featuring the chibi character Ichihime offering a snack reward. The Young Man, visible in an inset window, energetically explains the rules of forming sequences in mahjong, detailing how "4, 5, 6," "5, 6, 7," and "6, 7, 8" can complete sequences, but emphasizes that they must be "all the same suit," leading him to declare "A, the 4, 5, and 6 of bamboo" as the correct answer. The screen then transitions to a new question: 'Which of the following hands can Pon the West wind from another player?', with Ichihime now asking, 'Wanna have more snacks?' The Young Man continues his lively explanation, noting this question is "a little trickier" as it involves "honor tiles" and "Pon," which means completing a "triplet set." He excitedly identifies option "C" as the correct answer, exclaiming, "We need to complete a triplet set, baby!" The scene then shifts to a new tutorial slide titled 'A winning hand must contain a Yaku,' showing a 3D mahjong table. Ichihime visually explains that "This game would be too easy if that's all it takes to win. You must also have 'Yaku' to win a hand." The Young Man, leaning forward with a focused expression, dramatically introduces this "next topic" as a "doozy," humorously admitting he's "kind of been lying" by simplifying concepts, and is now "doling out the concepts one by one."

The video segment opens with a chalkboard-style tutorial screen titled 'A winning hand must contain a Yaku,' featuring a 3D-rendered mahjong table. The Young Man, visible in a small inset window in the bottom right, begins explaining in a clear, educational tone that a winning hand requires not only four sequences or triplets plus a pair, but also a 'Yaku,' which he defines as a win condition, drawing an analogy to poker terms like 'four of a kind' or 'full house.' As he speaks, the chibi-style anime character Ichihime appears on the left, visually reinforcing the rule that a 'No Yaku' notice will appear without one, while the Young Man reacts with expressive gestures, nodding and smiling. The scene then transitions to a new tutorial screen titled 'Simple Yaku: Yakuhai,' displaying examples of Yakuhai patterns including triplets of White, Green, and Red Dragon Tiles. The Young Man continues his explanation, detailing that triplets of any Dragon tiles constitute a Yaku, satisfying a win condition. He then introduces Prevalent Wind and Seat Wind Yakuhai, explaining that the middle of the table, currently showing 'East 2' with '24000' points, indicates the current round. He clarifies that if they have triplets of the Prevalent Wind (East Wind in this round), that also forms a Yaku. The Young Man points and reacts with animated curiosity and amusement as he follows the tutorial, before moving on to describe Seat Wind as being based on the player's sitting position.

The video opens on a 'Mahjong Soul' tutorial screen titled 'Simple Yaku: Yakuhai,' displaying examples of Dragon and Wind triplets. The Young Man, wearing a light pink button-up shirt and round glasses, explains in a clear, instructional voice that if "we're sitting in the West seat," a triplet of the West Wind satisfies a Yaku, fulfilling a "wind condition." The scene transitions to the 'Simple Yaku: Tanyao' screen, showing a hand of tiles and rules against terminal or honor tiles. The Young Man humorously renames Tanyao as "All Simps," comparing it to "Twitter replies of any famous streamer," and advises new players to "just try to get All Simps every time" as an easy win condition that helps learn game mechanics. Throughout these explanations, the chibi character Ichihime, with cat ears and a traditional outfit, is visible on the left, appearing to explain the concepts. The tutorial then presents a quiz: "Which of the following Sequences is a Yaku?" with options for wind triplets. The Young Man prompts viewers to "take a look at this" because "it's wind tiles, okay, it's honor tiles," acknowledging it's "a little tricky" and suggesting to "take a look at the round" for insight, all while reacting expressively with gestures and facial expressions in his cozy, blue-lit room.

The segment opens on a chalkboard-style Mahjong tutorial screen, displaying the quiz 'Which of the following Sequences is a Yaku?' with options A (three East Wind tiles), B (three South Wind tiles), and C (three West Wind tiles). Ichihime, a chibi-style anime character with cat ears, provides visual hints about Yaku referring to Prevalent Wind, Seat Wind, and Dragon Tiles, while a score of 'East 1' and '25000' points is visible. The Young Man, in an inset window, actively analyzes the question, stating in an engaging voice, "East one round right now. So, the East is looking pretty good as the prevalent wind," and then, "We're also sitting in the East seat. So, this is looking great for us right now because this is the seat wind and the prevalent wind. And in fact, A is going to be the correct answer for this one." The scene transitions to a new quiz, 'Which tile should you discard if you want to achieve Tanyao?', showing a hand of tiles and options A (1 of Bamboo), B (7 of Bamboo), and C (3 of Circles). Ichihime visually explains that Tanyao disallows Terminal or Honor tiles. The Young Man continues his analysis, asking, "Which of these tiles should you discard if you want to achieve Tanyao, aka all Simps?" He clarifies, "if you want all Simps, you can't have any ones, nines, or honor tiles," which include wind and dragon tiles. He then reviews the tile options, noting, "Pair twos, those seem great. Four, five, six of characters, that seems great. Three, four, that seems great." Upon seeing the '1 of Bamboo' option, he exclaims humorously, "One, two, three, excuse me, in this economy? Excuse me? Okay, that's a little sus." He concludes, "the only sus tile that you have in here is the one that you want to discard, and that's going to be the one of..." as the audio fades, all against a dark, grid-patterned digital background with ambient blue-purple lighting.

event_blocks.summary: On a chalkboard-style mahjong tutorial screen titled 'Simple Yaku: Riichi,' a Young Man, seated in a blue-purple lit room, enthusiastically explains the Riichi rule, describing it as what makes one feel like an anime protagonist. He clarifies that Riichi can be declared when a player achieves Tenpai without calling Chi, Pon, or Kan, meaning they are one tile away from a winning hand, exemplified by needing a two or five of circles to complete a hand of 2-6 Characters and 3-7 Bamboo. The declaration involves discarding a tile sideways, a visually 'anime' signal to others of being close to winning. Victory is achieved by drawing the needed tile or an opponent dealing it. Concurrently, the chibi-style anime character Ichihime observes cheerfully from the screen's left. The tutorial screen then transitions to a 'Yaku Overview,' where the Young Man explains 'Pure Straight' as a sequence of 1 through 9 in the same suit, highlighting the character suit, and describes the 'Mixed Triple Sequence' as having the same sequence across all three suits, like 1-2-3 in characters, circles, and bamboo, with the interface noting penalties like '-1 Han after making a call.' He further explains 'Pinfu,' requiring four sequences and a non-Yakuhai pair, with winning tiles from both sides of a sequence, and the 'Menzenchin Only' condition, meaning no 'pon' or 'chi' calls. The Young Man also details 'Half Flush,' achievable with one suit and honor tiles, calling it common, and 'Full Flush,' requiring an entire suit and being highly valuable. Throughout, he uses expressive hand gestures and maintains an animated, instructional demeanor, while the interface cycles through various yaku categories and their conditions, occasionally zooming in on tile examples.

Young Man.description: A young man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, speaking into a microphone with expressive gestures and facial expressions in a cozy, modern room with shelves, decorative items, and ambient blue-purple lighting. He provides clear, detailed explanations of mahjong yaku, clarifying complex rules like 'Half Flush' and 'Full Flush' with a conversational and occasionally humorous tone, noting their commonality and value.

Mahjong Tiles Tutorial Screen.description: An in-game tutorial screen with a chalkboard-style interface, displaying the 'Simple Yaku: Riichi' rule, two rows of mahjong tiles as examples, and explanatory text about the Riichi condition, with a 'Skip' button in the top right corner and a grid-patterned background with faint mahjong tile motifs.

Ichihime.description: A chibi-style anime character with brown hair, cat ears, red eyes, and a red-and-white traditional outfit, positioned on the left side of the screen, providing explanations with a cheerful demeanor and labeled 'Ichihime' in a red banner.

Yaku Overview.description: A detailed in-game tutorial interface with a dark blue background, displaying various yaku categories such as 'Half Flush,' 'Local Yaku: Pure Triple Chow,' and 'Full Flush,' each with tile examples and descriptions, including text like '-1 Han after making a call,' and tabs for different han levels at the top. The interface serves as a visual aid for the Young Man's explanations, showcasing specific yaku rules, their visual representation with tiles, and their associated han values, as he navigates through different categories.

raw_segments: The video displays a chalkboard-style mahjong game tutorial screen, titled 'Simple Yaku: Riichi,' which explains the Riichi rule. The Young Man, seated in a cozy, blue-purple lit room with shelves and decorative items, animatedly explains the rule in an enthusiastic voice, stating, "Alright! Let's talk about the Yaku that's in the name of Riichi Mahjong, Riichi," playfully calling it "the one that makes you feel like an anime protagonist." He elaborates that a player can declare Riichi if they achieve Tenpai without having called Chi, Pon, or Kan, a condition also displayed on the screen above example mahjong tiles. He clarifies that the player must be "one tile away from having a winning hand," referencing the displayed tile examples (2-6 Characters, 3-7 Bamboo, West, East, West, West, and a 3 Circles) by noting, "All we need to finish it is either a two of circles or a five of circles." He describes the declaration action as discarding a tile sideways, calling it "very anime, very cool," which signals to others, "hey, I'm one tile away from winning, so be careful." He concludes by explaining that victory comes if an opponent deals the needed tile or if the player draws it themselves on their turn, all while the chibi-style anime character Ichihime, with brown hair, cat ears, red eyes, and a red-and-white traditional outfit, observes with a cheerful expression from the left side of the screen, which features a dark, grid-patterned background with faint mahjong tile motifs and a 'Skip' button.

The segment opens with the 'Mahjong Tiles Tutorial Screen' displaying the 'Simple Yaku: Riichi' rule, featuring a chalkboard-style interface with mahjong tiles and explanatory text. The chibi-style character 'Ichihime' is on the left, labeled with her name, offering a cheerful explanation. In the bottom right, the 'Young Man' is visible in a small inset window, speaking into a microphone with expressive hand gestures, explaining the downside of Riichi: "Once you call Riichi, on your turn, you draw a tile, and if it's not the tile that you need, you have to discard it, no matter how valuable it is." He adds that Riichi will be covered in an example game. The screen then transitions to a detailed 'Yaku Overview' interface, showcasing various yaku categories like 'Pure Straight' and 'Mixed Triple Sequence,' accompanied by upbeat, whimsical background music. The 'Young Man' continues his clear, instructional explanation, gesturing towards the screen. He elaborates on 'Pure Straight' as "a hand with a complete sequence of 1 through 9" of the same suit, exemplified by the character suit, which is highlighted on the screen. He then introduces the "less common" and "very confusing" 'Mixed Triple Sequence,' clarifying it as having "the same sequence on all three suits," such as "1, 2, 3 in characters, 1, 2, 3 in circles, and 1, 2, 3 in bamboo," as the interface highlights specific yaku with text like '-1 Han after making a call.' The camera occasionally zooms in on specific tile examples, like the 'Pure Straight' sequence, before returning to the full interface, while the 'Young Man' maintains an engaged demeanor, nodding and using hand motions to emphasize his points.

The segment opens with a young man, wearing round glasses and a light pink button-up shirt, speaking into a microphone from a cozy, modern room with blue-purple lighting, as he introduces the 'Pinfu' yaku on a detailed Mahjong 'Yaku Overview' tutorial screen. He explains that Pinfu requires four sequences and one non-Yakuhai pair, emphasizing that winning tiles must be from both sides of a sequence, stating, "Again, very confusing." He then simplifies, clarifying, "Pinfu means you need four sequences. No triplets in this one, okay?" The screen zooms in on tile examples, showing sequences like 1-2-3 Man, 4-5-6 Pin, and 7-8-9 Sou, as he elaborates on the 'double-sided wait' condition, illustrating with examples like waiting on the five or eight of bamboo, contrasting it with a single-sided wait. He summarizes the Pinfu requirements, then reads the 'Menzenchin Only' condition, humorously remarking that it "sounds like the name of a character you'd get a blessing from in Elden Ring," before explaining it means no 'pon' or 'chi' calls. Throughout, he uses expressive hand gestures, pointing and nodding, maintaining an animated and informative demeanor, as the interface displays various yaku categories and their conditions, including 'Pure Double Sequence' and 'Half Flush,' before he transitions to discuss the latter.

The segment opens on a dark blue 'Yaku Overview' tutorial interface from a digital mahjong game, displaying tabs for various han levels and cycling through yaku like 'Half Flush' and 'Local Yaku: Pure Triple Chow.' A Young Man, with short black hair and round glasses, wearing a light pink button-up shirt, appears in an inset in the bottom right, speaking into a microphone with expressive gestures and facial expressions from a cozy, modern room with blue-purple lighting. He explains the 'Half Flush' yaku, detailing how to win with tiles from one of the three Number Tiles and Honor Tiles, clarifying, "Basically, you can only have one suit of tiles and then you can finish the rest of your hand with honor tiles, however you'd like." He points out the visual example of tiles in the character suit alongside winds and dragons, describing it as "a very, very common hand" while humorously admitting, "I haven't been there in a while. No, just kidding." The tutorial screen notes a '-1 Han after making a call' penalty. The Young Man then guides the viewer as the interface navigates to the '6 Han' tab, revealing the 'Full Flush' yaku. He explains that 'Full Flush' requires winning with "only one suit," emphasizing, "this is way hard, but it's very, very valuable." He briefly touches on how yaku value is determined by 'han' before the segment concludes with him reiterating the 'Full Flush' requirement of "entirely one suit."

event_blocks.summary: The Young Man begins by explaining the 'Full Flush' yaku on a tutorial screen, then transitions to the game's main menu, guiding viewers on setting up a match against AI. He navigates to the 'Create Room' screen, configuring a 4-Player vs AI match with a 300-second thinking time and disabled advanced settings, followed by an audible click to create the room. As a loading cutscene plays, he comments on the characters before the 'Match Start' screen appears, leading into gameplay on a 3D mahjong table. During the game, identified as 'Yu_song' in the South position against three 'AI(Easy)' opponents in the 'East 1' round with 25000 points, he explains his strategy for 'all Simps' (Tanyao), discarding honor tiles and 'ones, nines'. He discards a 'West' tile, and the game progresses with AI discards. Upon drawing a '4 of circles', he notes the 'Chii' option for a sequence. Later, he calls 'Chii' to form a sequence with a 3 and 5 of circles, confirmed by an Ichihime animation, revealing his tiles for 'Tanyao'. He discards a tile, and the game continues with AI turns. He discusses strategy, emphasizing flexibility over fixating on a specific yaku. He explains the 'Chii' mechanic, noting the player on his left discarded a 'three of circles' which allowed him to form a sequence. He rejects a 'three of circles' tile, opting to 'call skip' to maintain his hand. Drawing another bamboo tile, he continues to discard honor tiles. He reacts with excitement as an AI discards a 'five of bamboo', calling it 'delicious'. He contemplates calling 'Chii' for a 5-6-7 sequence, which he then executes. He strategizes about discarding a 'Red Dragon' tile to focus on sequences, and later draws a 'four of circles', which he excitedly places next to a 'three of characters' to form another sequence, noting a pair of 'seven of characters'. He contemplates discarding a 'two of circles'. He expresses surprise when an AI calls 'Riichi', indicating it's one tile away from winning, and stresses caution. Despite the risk, he decides to continue playing to complete his hand. He analyzes sequences like '5, 6, 7' and compares tile values. He explains the 'Riichi' rule, stating that previously discarded tiles become safe discards. He acknowledges that some discards might seem unusual but aims to teach basic mechanics. He exclaims 'Wow! Like great luck!' upon drawing a tile to complete a '5-6-7 sequence', making him 'one tile away from winning'. He confidently discards the '7 of circles', anticipating a win if an opponent deals or he draws a 'two of characters' or 'five of characters'. He skips a 'Chii' option for a 4 of bamboo, deeming it needless. He considers using a 'seven of characters' as a triplet or pair, noting the need for a pair to complete his hand. He expresses hope to win a game in his tutorial and admits to feeling nervous as the game progresses, with the multiplier decreasing significantly and the 'Left' counter nearing zero.

Loading Cutscene.description: An animated sequence showing four characters playing table tennis in a traditional Japanese setting, with a progress bar and text 'Sorting out the table...' at the bottom, which the Young Man's voice comments on as "Wholesome husbandos and waifus shown," before transitioning to 'Match Start' in glowing script, accompanied by a distinct game sound effect.

Main Menu Screen.description: A vibrant, animated game menu with a cherry blossom background, a traditional Japanese building, and a character named Yu_song (a girl with cat ears, red eyes, and a red-and-white kimono) on the left, offering options like 'Ranked Match,' 'Tournament Match,' and 'Friendly,' through which the Young Man's voice guides the user with audible clicks.

Create Room Screen.description: A configuration interface for setting up a mahjong match, featuring options for Mode, Length, Thinking Time, and Advanced settings, where the Young Man's voice instructs on specific selections like '4-Player vs AI' and '300+0s' thinking time, accompanied by audible clicks.

Room Screen.description: A waiting room interface showing the player Yu_song as the host with a 'Ready' status, a 'Start' button, and match details, set against a backdrop of a traditional Japanese room, where the Young Man's voice instructs the user to press 'Start' with an audible click.

Yaku Overview.description: A detailed in-game tutorial interface with a dark blue background, displaying various yaku categories, including 'Full Flush' with tile examples and descriptions like '-1 Han after making a call,' which the Young Man's voice explains in detail.

Young Man.description: A young man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, speaking animatedly into a microphone with expressive gestures and facial expressions (smiling, nodding, raising his hands, displaying concentration and excitement) from a cozy, modern room with shelves, decorative items, and ambient blue-purple lighting. He vocalizes his mahjong strategy, discussing tile discards and optimal waits, explaining his confidence in a 'safe discard' due to an opponent being in 'richi,' and then correcting himself. His voice conveys a range of emotions, from analytical and confident to pleading and slightly nervous as he expresses his strong desire to win a game for his tutorial.

3D Mahjong Table.description: A 3D-rendered overhead view of a mahjong table with tiles arranged in walls, set on a teal playing surface with black borders, indicating the start of gameplay, introduced with a distinct game sound effect.

In-Game Mahjong Interface.description: A digital mahjong game interface with a blue table, player avatars labeled 'AI(Easy)' and 'Yu_song' at the four corners, a hand of tiles at the bottom (including 3, 4, 7, and 5 of circles, 7 of bamboo, and 5 of characters, with a highlighted 5 of circles tile), a central score display showing 'East 1' and '25000' points (fluctuating to '24000'), and the number '293' on the right side of the screen. The discard pile grows as tiles are played, and the 'Left' counter updates from 3 to 2, then to 1, and finally to 0. The central multiplier decreases from 'x18' to 'x14' and then 'x10', and a '2 Left' or '4 Left' indicator updates above the discard pile, reflecting the game state that the Young Man is actively analyzing and reacting to.

Ichihime.description: A chibi-style anime character with brown hair, cat ears, red eyes, and a red-and-white traditional outfit, positioned on the left side of the screen. She appears briefly as an animation to confirm the player's 'Chii' call, as described by the player as 'your character pops up' and 'you reveal your characters'.

raw_segments: The video opens with a tutorial screen titled 'Yaku Overview,' displaying the 'Full Flush' yaku with mahjong tiles and a note about losing 1 Han after a call. A Young Man, visible in an inset, speaks into a microphone with expressive gestures, explaining the yaku rules, stating, "No dragons, just one suit... you can do this with a character suit... or the circle suit or the bamboo suit." He then announces, "Okay, let's do an example game," as the scene transitions to the game's vibrant main menu, featuring cherry blossoms, a traditional Japanese building, and the character Yu_song. The Young Man continues to speak, guiding the viewer through the menu options, explaining, "this screen is what you're going to see basically... when you log in and create an account... for the first time and let me show you how to set up a game versus AI at first so you don't have to jump into ranked match immediately." He then clicks on 'Friendly Match' with an audible click, instructing, "You're going to want to create create room." On the 'Create Room' screen, he configures a 4-Player vs AI match, advising to "crank that up to 300" for thinking time (300+0s), and to leave advanced settings "disabled." After clicking 'Create' (another audible click), the room is set up with Yu_song as the host, showing a 'Ready' status. As an animated cutscene of characters playing table tennis loads, the Young Man comments, "Wholesome husbandos and waifus shown," before exclaiming, "All right, let's get into it." A distinct game sound effect plays as the 'Match Start' screen appears, leading into the gameplay on a 3D-rendered mahjong table, displaying the player's hand, AI opponents, and a central score of 'East 1' and 25000 points. The Young Man remains engaged, reacting to the game's progression, concluding with "Great."

The segment opens with a Young Man, wearing round glasses and a light pink button-up shirt, actively speaking into a microphone with expressive gestures and facial expressions in his cozy, blue-purple lit streaming room, featuring shelves and a keyboard. He introduces the digital mahjong gameplay, stating, "Great starting position. Let's talk about what we're seeing here," as the 'Yu_song' player at the South position faces three 'AI(Easy)' opponents. He explains they are in the 'East 1' round with 25000 points and a multiplier decreasing from x68 to x61, outlining his strategy to go for "all Simps" by discarding "ones, nines, and um all these honor tiles." The player's hand, featuring various circles, bamboo, and wind tiles, is visible at the bottom of the blue game table. As he describes the draw-and-discard mechanic, the player discards a 'West' tile, followed by the North AI discarding a 'Green' and the West AI a 'West'. The Young Man then exclaims, "Wow! Okay, so this is awesome. This is really cool," as the player draws a '4 of circles'. The game interface, displaying 'Friendly Match - 4-Player (vs AI)' in the top-left and the number '293' on the right, highlights a 'Chii' option for a possible sequence with the 3 and 5 of circles, prompting him to question, "So, why do we have the option to chi here, right? Okay, we have the four and six..."

The video segment opens with a live gameplay session of a digital mahjong game, viewed from an overhead perspective on a blue table. The interface displays four player positions, with avatars labeled 'AI(Easy)' and 'Yu_song', the latter being the player whose hand of tiles (including 3, 7, 1 circles; 2, 4, 5, 6, 7 bamboo; South, Green, Red Dragon) is visible at the bottom. The central score display shows 'East 1' with 25000 points and a multiplier of x61, which decreases to x54. The player, Yu_song, whose voice narrates the gameplay, explains that an opponent discarded the five of circles, which is exactly what he needs to call 'Chii'. He decides to "showcase how we do it" and calls 'Chii'. Visually, a chibi-style character with brown hair and cat ears, Ichihime, briefly appears as an animation to confirm the action, aligning with Yu_song's narration that "your character pops up" and "you reveal your characters." On-screen text confirms, '*You reveal your tiles!'. Yu_song explains that everyone at the table can now see his revealed tiles. He then exclaims, "all simps, baby, all simps ride or die," connecting to the on-screen text '*Tanyao is just another name for all simps!'. He discards a tile, and AI opponents take their turns. Yu_song, seen in a small inset window in his cozy, blue-purple lit room, adjusts his glasses and continues to speak into his microphone, commenting on a 'Southwind' tile ("you hate to see it") but reiterating "we're going for all simps here." He elaborates on real Mahjong strategy, emphasizing not to be "tunnel visioned on a specific Yaku" but to "go with the flow." He then explains why he could call Chii, stating the player on his left discarded the three of circles, and he holds the one and two, anticipating the viewer's thought of being "one step closer to making a complete hand" before warning them to "watch out 'cause."

The segment opens with the Young Man, visible in an inset window, providing clear and analytical commentary on a live 'Mahjong Soul' gameplay session. He explains his strategy to achieve 'Tanyao,' noting that calling 'Chii' on a tile would lock in a 1-2-3 sequence and prevent a 'Yaku,' stating, "We're going for all simps here, okay?" He rejects a 'three of circles' tile, deciding to "call skip here" as the digital mahjong interface displays 'Yu_song's evolving hand with various circles and bamboo tiles. The central score display updates from a x54 multiplier to x53. He reacts positively to drawing a bamboo tile, exclaiming, "Great. Awesome. Drawing another bamboo tile. Let's let's keep cutting our honors." As the game progresses, the multiplier drops to x50. He observes the AI opponents' discards, reacting with an excited "Wow! Wow, wow, wow!" when an 'AI(Easy)' player discards a 'five of bamboo,' which he interprets as "feeding us delicious tiles." He then analyzes his own hand, noting "five, six, six, seven of bamboo," and humorously contemplates calling 'Chii' to "get some brunch with a mimosa," before explaining that doing so would complete a 5-6-7 sequence, which he then executes, all while gesturing expressively in his softly lit room.

The video opens with a continuous gameplay session of a digital mahjong game, viewed from the perspective of the player 'Yu_song' at the bottom of the screen. The interface displays a blue mahjong table with four player positions, featuring 'AI(Easy)' avatars and 'Yu_song's' avatar. The central score reads 'East 1' with 25000 points, and a multiplier counter, starting at x50, steadily decreases. In a small inset window, a Young Man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt is visible, speaking into a microphone with expressive facial movements and hand gestures, reacting to the gameplay from a room with shelves and blue-purple lighting. He immediately engages in strategic commentary, noting, "we still have five six. So, if this player discards a four or a seven, we can still make a sequence just like that." He then decides, "all Sims, we're tunneling, let's cut this red dragon," as 'Yu_song' discards a '中' (Red Dragon) tile from their hand, which includes '三萬', '七萬', '一萬', '四萬', and various bamboo and circle tiles. As 'Yu_song' draws a '一萬', then a '七筒', and an 'AI(Easy)' opponent discards a '一萬', the Young Man exclaims, "Wow, seven. Okay, that's new. That's new," reacting to the drawn '七筒', adding, "I'm kinda liking the look of that seven." He continues strategizing, stating, "in order to finish this hand, we're going to need to cut this one eventually," and decides, "let's do it now because we can't have ones, nines, or any of these honor tiles." As 'Yu_song' draws a '四萬' and another 'AI(Easy)' opponent discards a '七筒', the Young Man excitedly declares, "Four! Woo! Okay, this is looking great. This is looking great 'cause that goes right next to our three of characters." He highlights the progress, "now we can make another sequence based off of that. And we're one step closer to finishing our hand," and adds, "we even have a pair of the seven of characters. This is looking phenomenal." The multiplier counter decreases to x37, and the number '293' on the right updates, as the Young Man concludes, "Let's cut this two of circles for now."

The segment features a continuous gameplay session of a digital mahjong game, viewed from the perspective of player 'Yu_song' at the bottom of the screen. The interface displays a blue mahjong table with 'AI(Easy)' opponents and a central score of 'East 1' with '25000' points, briefly dipping to '24000'. The player's hand, neatly arranged, includes tiles like 3, 4, 7, and 5 of circles, 7 of bamboo, and 5 of characters, with a highlighted 5 of circles tile. In the bottom-right, a Young Man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink shirt is seen in an inset window, actively reacting to the game. He exclaims "Ooh! Wow!" with excitement, gesturing and speaking into a microphone, asking "Are you feeling it, Mr. Krabs?" He analyzes the game, expressing surprise that the AI "just called Riichi," explaining that the character's voice and a sideways tile discard confirm it, meaning the AI is "one tile away from winning." He emphasizes the need to be "very, very careful" not to drop the tile the AI is waiting for. Despite the risk, he decides to "throw caution to the wind" and "keep playing to complete our hand," given it's an AI game. He then meticulously analyzes his own hand, contemplating sequences like "5, 6, 7" and the strategic value of specific tiles like the 5 and 7 of circles versus the 5 and 6 of bamboo, all while displaying expressive facial movements, smiles, and nods against a blue-purple lit background.

The video showcases a continuous digital mahjong gameplay session from the perspective of player 'Yu_song,' whose hand of tiles, including 3, 4, 7, and 5 of circles, 7 of bamboo, and 5 of characters, is visible at the bottom, with a 5 of circles tile highlighted. The blue mahjong table features three 'AI(Easy)' opponents, while the central score display shows 'East 1' at 25000 points, fluctuating to 24000, and a static '293' on the right. In a bottom-right inset, a Young Man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt speaks expressively into a microphone from a cozy, blue-purple lit room. He provides a dynamic commentary, explaining mahjong strategy, such as how to complete sequences with specific tiles like a 4 or 7 of bamboo, or a 6 of circles, and comparing the odds of different tile draws. He states, "Let's do this to broaden our chances," as the player discards a 5 of circles. Reacting to a subsequent draw, he exclaims, "Wow, bad luck," and explains the game mechanic of discarding a tile only to draw another immediately. He then delves into the 'Riichi' rule, clarifying that once declared, players cannot win off any tiles they've previously discarded, making those "safe discards." He applies this to the current game, noting that since one 5 of circles was safe, another would also be safe as the opponent didn't win on it. As the player discards a 2 of circles, he acknowledges that some discards might appear "weird" to experienced players but emphasizes his goal is to teach the basics. Throughout, he maintains engaging facial expressions, smiling, nodding, raising his hands, and occasionally adjusting his glasses, reflecting his deep involvement and educational approach.

The video opens with a continuous gameplay session of a digital mahjong game, viewed from the perspective of player 'Yu_song' at the bottom of the screen, facing three 'AI(Easy)' opponents. The blue game table displays walls of tiles, discarded tiles, and the current round information: 'East 1' with a score fluctuating between 25000 and 24000, and a multiplier decreasing from x25 to x18. The player's hand includes various circles, bamboos, and character tiles. Throughout, the Young Man, identified as 'Yu_song', is seen in a bottom-right inset, wearing a light pink shirt and round glasses, speaking into a microphone in a blue-purple lit room. He actively comments on his strategy, stating, "let's keep cutting and let's keep aiming to win with the sequences that we have." At 0:06, he exclaims "Wow! Like great luck!" and explains that they just drew a tile to complete a "5-6-7 sequence," making them "one tile away from winning." At 0:13, a pop-up confirms this, showing '二萬' and '伍萬' as required tiles with '2 Left' and '4 Left' respectively, as the Young Man gestures expressively. He confidently states they are "100% doing" a discard of the 7 of circles, anticipating a win if an opponent deals the "two of characters or the five of characters," or if he draws it himself, which he calls "dope." At 0:32, an AI discards a tile, and the player is presented with 'Chii' and 'Skip' options, with a green circle highlighting the 4 of bamboo. The Young Man explains he'll "skip this one, this seems a little needless" because they already have a sequence, before discarding a 7 of circles. He continues to react dynamically, nodding, smiling, and gesturing, further explaining strategic options, such as using the 7 of characters as a triplet instead of a pair, and noting, "we need a pair to complete."

The segment opens with a digital mahjong game in progress, viewed from the perspective of player 'Yu_song' at the bottom of a blue table, facing three 'AI(Easy)' opponents. The central score display shows 'East 1' with points fluctuating from 25000 down to 24000, while a '293' indicator remains static on the right. The player's hand, featuring multiple 7s of circles, 3, 4, 5, 6 of circles, and 3, 4, 5 of characters, has a 5 of circles tile highlighted. In the bottom right, a Young Man with short black hair and round glasses, wearing a light pink shirt, actively speaks into a microphone from a blue-purple lit room with shelves. He gestures expressively, explaining his strategy: initially considering discarding a 'four of characters' or 'three of characters' to form a pair, then deciding to 'drop this seven of characters' to wait on 'two and the five' because it offers more options. As the player draws and discards, the discard pile grows, and the 'Left' counter decreases from 3 to 2, then to 1, and finally to 0, with a '2 Left' or '4 Left' indicator updating above the pile. The central multiplier also drops from 'x18' to 'x14' and then 'x10'. The Young Man, showing concentration and excitement, notes that a drawn tile 'doesn't help very much' but confidently discards it, explaining that an opponent in 'richi' cannot win off it, making it a '100% a safe discard,' though he quickly corrects himself, acknowledging other players could still win. He then pleads, 'Oh my god, please. It would be so cool if I could win a game in my mahjong tutorial,' his voice filled with hope, before questioning, 'Why am I getting a little nervous?' as he continues to react dynamically to the focused gameplay.

event_blocks.summary: The In-Game Mahjong Interface displays a 4-player friendly match with the player 'Yu_song' and three 'AI(Easy)' opponents. The Young Man, in the bottom-right inset, explains that the 'Left' counter, initially at 3, signifies remaining tiles in the wall, with zero indicating the round's end, and expresses a desire to win before then. He instructs to discard a 5 of circles from his hand, which contains circles, bamboo, and characters. The 'Left' counter decreases to 2, then 1, as the central multiplier drops from 'x18' to 'x14'. He opts to skip a 'chii' as unnecessary and discards an eight of circles. When the 'Left' counter reaches 0 and the multiplier falls to 'x10', the Young Man exclaims in excitement, noting that with no characters left in the wall, the AI opponents on the last turn dealt the necessary tile. He urges to press the 'Ron' button to win. A 'Ron' call initiates a celebratory animation, prompting the Young Man to clap and cheer. The screen transitions to results showing 'Yu_song' winning with 29900 points, ranked 2nd. He then begins explaining the winning yaku, identifying 'Under the River 1 Han', 'All Simples 1 Han', and 'Red Five 1 Han', totaling 3 Han and 30 Fu, with 'Under the River' described as a 'cherry on top' signifying a win on the very last tile.

Young Man.description: A young man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, seen in a cozy, modern room with blue-purple lighting. He speaks into a microphone with an excited, high-energy voice, explaining game mechanics like the 'tiles remaining in the wall' and strategizing plays ('drop this five', 'skip this chii'). He reacts with immense joy and loud exclamations ('Wow! Oh my god! I love Mahjong, baby!') upon winning, raising his hands, adjusting his glasses, and gesturing animatedly. He claps loudly and cheers, then begins to explain the winning yaku, specifically 'Under the River'.

In-Game Mahjong Interface.description: A digital mahjong game interface with a blue table, player avatars labeled 'AI(Easy)' and 'Yu_song', a hand of tiles at the bottom, and a central score display. The 'Left' counter decreases from 3 to 0, and the central multiplier drops from 'x18' to 'x10'. The player discards a 5 of circles, calls 'Chii' with a green highlight on a 4 of bamboo, and then calls 'Ron' as the final tile is drawn, triggering a celebratory animation. The game mechanics are explained by the Young Man, who notes the 'tiles remaining in the wall' and the significance of the 'last turn' for the win. The results screen shows 'Yu_song' winning with 29900 points, ranked 2nd, and displays the winning hand with yaku details: 'Under the River 1 Han', 'All Simples 1 Han', 'Red Five 1 Han', totaling 3 Han and 30 Fu, which the Young Man begins to explain.

raw_segments: The video opens on a digital mahjong game interface, displaying a 4-player friendly match with three 'AI(Easy)' opponents and the player 'Yu_song'. The Young Man, visible in the bottom-right inset, explains that the 'Left' counter, initially at 3, indicates "how many tiles are remaining in the wall," and that reaching zero signifies the end of the round. He expresses his desire to win before that, then instructs to "drop this five" as the player's hand, featuring circles, bamboo, and characters, highlights a 5 of circles. The 'Left' counter decreases to 2, then 1, while the central multiplier drops from 'x18' to 'x14'. He decides to "skip this chii 'cause it's kind of unneeded" and then to "drop this eight of a... eight of circles." raw_segments: The video captures a young man, with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, deeply engrossed in a digital mahjong 'Friendly Match - 4-Player (vs AI)'. He speaks into a microphone from his cozy, blue-purple lit room, explaining, "now it's the new round. It's still the East round," and that he's "sitting in the East seat." The game interface, featuring a blue table, displays his avatar 'Yu_song' at the bottom and three 'AI(Easy)' opponents. The central score reads 'East 2' with a multiplier decreasing from 'x69' to 'x61' and a score of 29900, while a static '293' is visible on the right. He outlines his strategy, stating, "Let's go for a Riichi hand," but notes, "we can't call Chi or Pon unfortunately," adding, "we kind of have to just play with ourselves." As the game progresses, AI opponents discard '北' (North), '西' (West), and '東' (East) tiles into the growing discard pile. The young man, adjusting his glasses and gesturing expressively, explains his tile management: "I'm just going to cut some of these honor tiles to begin with because... honor tiles, you can only make triplets of them, right? You can't make sequences." He then discards a '1筒' (1 Tong) tile from his hand, which includes various tiles like 1, 3, 7, 9 of '萬', 1, 2, 4, 4 of '筒', 4, 6, 7, 8 of '索', and the wind tiles '南' and '北'. He assesses his hand as "in decent shape" and reacts positively to a draw, exclaiming, "that's a nice draw." He reiterates his goal to "assemble a winning hand as fast as possible," adding a self-aware disclaimer about his "expert rank" for those seeking "the most efficiently" play. Throughout, the 'Left' counter on the interface steadily decreases from 69 to 61, reflecting the ongoing turns.

The minute opens on a digital mahjong game, viewed from the perspective of player 'Yu_song' at the bottom of a blue table, surrounded by three 'AI(Easy)' opponents. In the bottom-right inset, a Young Man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt speaks into his microphone, explaining his approach to the game, stating he can't play "100% hyper-efficiently always," and his motivation for creating Mahjong videos due to a lack of English-speaking content creators. As the game progresses, the central score display shows 'East 2' with points around 29900, and the 'Left' counter and multiplier steadily decrease from x61 to x45. The Young Man discusses strategy, suggesting to "cut this one of circles" and considering sequences like "1-2-3" or "2-3-4" to keep options open. Visually, the player's hand, featuring circles, bamboo, and character tiles, is rearranged, and a highlighted 5 of circles tile is discarded, followed by a 4 of circles and later a 7 of circles, while AI opponents discard tiles like 4 of bamboo, 5 of bamboo, and 9 of characters, causing the discard piles to grow. The Young Man reacts with an excited "Wow! Okay, what great luck!" upon a "4-5-6 sequence" being completed, then deliberates on finishing other sequences. As a 'Chii' option briefly appears on screen, he explains the strategic implications, noting that calling 'Chii' would prevent a 'Riichi' call, leading him to decide to "skip here." He concludes by observing a "3, 4" situation, remarking that this type of scenario will be seen "a lot." Throughout, he maintains expressive facial reactions and hand gestures, adjusting his glasses and raising his hands, all within his cozy, blue-purple lit room.

The video captures a continuous digital mahjong game from the 'Yu_song' player's perspective, set on a blue table with 'AI(Easy)' opponents. In the bottom right, the Young Man, wearing glasses and a pink shirt, actively comments into his microphone from his blue and purple lit room. He explains that a 'shiny' tile, like the 'one' he observes in his hand, is 'extra valuable this round,' though he pretends it's not shiny for educational purposes. He strategizes to improve his hand, noting that with 'this one and the three,' he's 'just waiting on the two of characters.' He decides to discard the 'one,' which he does, causing the central multiplier to decrease from x45 to x41 and then to x37, and the 'Left' counter to drop from 45 to 37. His hand, featuring tiles like 3, 4, 7, and 5 of circles, 7 of bamboo, and 5 of characters, updates as he draws and discards, with the 'Chii' option briefly appearing. He expresses regret, stating, 'If we had kept that nine, we would have 789 right now, and I'd be laughing,' smiling and adjusting his glasses. Dismissing the 'sunk cost fallacy,' he then excitedly declares his hand 'beautiful' and 'gorgeous' after drawing the 'six of bamboo,' raising his hands in reaction. The discard piles grow incrementally in the center and on the right side of the table, while the 'East 2' score display and the static '293' remain visible.

The segment opens with a continuous gameplay session of a digital mahjong game, viewed from the perspective of player 'Yu_song' at a blue table against three 'AI(Easy)' opponents. The player's hand, featuring various circles, bamboo, and character tiles, is visible at the bottom, with a 5 of circles tile highlighted. The Young Man, situated in the bottom right inset with a cozy, blue-purple lit room background, provides live commentary, speaking into a microphone with expressive gestures. He begins by analyzing tile sequences, noting "4 5 6 of bamboo and 6 7 8," and suggesting to "take apart the 7." As the 'Left' counter updates from 69 to 61, then to 45, and the central multiplier decreases, the player discards a 1, 2, and then a 3 of circles. The 'Chii' option briefly appears after each discard, accompanied by distinct game sounds, prompting the Young Man to explain, "We could pawn here, but again we're going for Riichi, so we're going to skip for now." He then exclaims "Wow!" as another 'Chii' option appears, elaborating on the strategic choice: "in a real game, you could Chii here. You could finish your 2 3 4 sequence, and look at that, our hand is in a great shape for all simps, right?" This coincides with the on-screen text '*You could Chii and pivot to all simps!'. He clarifies, "But we're not going to do that for the sake of trying to show you what Riichi is like." Suddenly, a game sound indicates an AI declaration, and the Young Man reacts with a frustrated "Ah! You beat me to it, Easy AI! You beat me to it." He explains, "So this AI called Riichi... They're one tile away from winning," emphasizing, "obviously, we don't want to give him that winning tile." He continues to analyze the discard pile, stating, "all the tiles that they've discarded so far are safe to discard," before concluding that "this nine is not safe." Throughout, the Young Man remains highly engaged, smiling, nodding, raising his hands, and adjusting his glasses, while game sounds accompany tile placements and option prompts.

The video displays a continuous overhead gameplay session of a digital Riichi Mahjong game, featuring a blue table with the player 'Yu_song' at the bottom and three 'AI(Easy)' opponents. The Young Man, visible in a bottom-right inset, actively comments on the game, explaining strategic decisions like why "nines are safer to discard" and analyzing opponent moves, noting "Eastwind, they've discarded before. The central score shows 'East 2' and fluctuating points around 29900, while the 'Left' counter steadily decreases from 21 to 5, accompanied by subtle game sounds of tiles being drawn and discarded. The Young Man reacts to drawing "another three," expressing "Oh man," and reflecting on "the one that got away." He then considers discarding a highlighted 5 of circles, explaining that "fives generally seem good" but his current hand doesn't benefit, concluding, "it's a safe discard. So let's get rid of it." He discards the 5 of circles, then a 5 of bamboo, a 6 of bamboo, and a 3 of circles, with the 'Chii' option briefly appearing after the first discard. He reacts to drawing a "Red Dragon," lamenting, "not helping that much. Oh man." As the 'Riichi' and 'Skip' options appear after his final discard, the Young Man exclaims "WOAH baby! What incredible luck! What incredible luck!" despite earlier accepting he might not reach Riichi, now stating, "we might not win this round, but at least I can show you what going into Riichi is like." Throughout, he maintains expressive gestures, smiling, nodding, raising his hands, and adjusting his glasses in his cozy, blue-lit room.
